title: Dyeing with turmeric
subtitle: A substantive golden yellow on wool and silk using grocery store turmeric powder
excerpt: Turmeric is one of the most accessible natural dyes: it bonds directly to protein fibres without a mordant, produces a strong golden yellow with grocery store powder, and requires only a gentle simmer. The colour has low lightfastness and will fade with repeated washing and light exposure, making it better suited to decorative items than everyday use.
sourceNotes: Turmeric as a substantive dye for protein fibres is consistent across UK and US natural dye guild instruction. Lightfastness limitations are documented in Jenny Dean's 'Wild Colour' and in Sasha Duerr's work. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A natural dye that bonds to protein fibres without requiring a separate mordant. Turmeric is substantive on wool and silk; it produces good colour depth with no pre-treatment, though the bond is weaker than mordant-assisted dyes and fades more readily with light and washing.

A mineral or plant compound used to fix natural dye to fibre by forming a molecular bridge between the dye molecule and the fibre. Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is the most common mordant. Substantive dyes such as turmeric bind without a mordant, but mordanting turmeric with alum can improve colour depth slightly.

The resistance of a dyed colour to fading when exposed to light. Turmeric has low lightfastness: the yellow will visibly fade within weeks of exposure to strong sunlight. Items dyed with turmeric are best kept out of direct light and washed in cool water.

content.text: is required for it to bond to wool or silk, and the process is straightforward enough for a first natural dye project. The limitation is

: turmeric fades noticeably in strong light and with repeated washing. It is well suited to decorative items, cushion covers, or yarn for projects that will not be worn daily. Turmeric powder from a kitchen cupboard works; there is no need for specialist dyeing-grade material. The Herbal Apothecary category covers turmeric as a culinary spice and medicinal plant if you want to explore its non-dyeing uses.

attrs.title: Equipment safety
attrs.body: Use separate pots and utensils for all natural dyeing. Never use dye equipment for food preparation afterwards.

items.name: Large stainless steel or enamel dye pot

content.text: Soak the wool or silk in warm water for at least 30 minutes until fully saturated. Do not wring; lift gently when moving to the dye pot. Turmeric does not require a pre-mordant bath; go directly to the dye step.

Dissolve the turmeric powder in a small amount of warm water to form a paste, then add it to the dye pot with 4 litres of water. Stir to disperse evenly. Add the pre-wetted fibre to the dye pot while the water is still cool, then heat gradually to 70°C over approximately 20 minutes. Keep the temperature between 70°C and 80°C for 45 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes. Overheating wool above 85°C causes felting; silk should not exceed 80°C.

Remove the fibre from the dye pot and allow it to cool slightly before rinsing. Rinse in water at the same temperature as the dye bath to avoid shocking the fibre, then rinse again in progressively cooler water until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently and hang to dry away from direct sunlight. The turmeric yellow deepens as the fibre dries.

items.problem: The colour is very uneven with dark patches and pale areas
items.cause: The fibre was not fully wetted before entering the dye bath, or was not moved regularly during dyeing
items.fix: Soak fibre thoroughly until no dry spots remain and no air bubbles rise when it is submerged. Stir gently every few minutes during dyeing to ensure all areas have equal contact with the dye liquid.

items.problem: The colour fades to near-nothing after the first wash
items.cause: The dye bath temperature was too low and the turmeric did not bond well, or the wash temperature was too hot
items.fix: Ensure the dye bath reaches and holds 70 to 75°C for a full 45 minutes. Always wash turmeric-dyed items in cool water (under 30°C) using a gentle detergent to reduce colour loss.
